subject: Tauri desktop E2E
claim: Tauri now recommends WebdriverIO with @wdio/tauri-service for Windows, Linux, and macOS, with embedded WebDriver as the default path and the mechanism that enables macOS.
evidence_quote: The recommended way to use it with Tauri is WebdriverIO and the @wdio/tauri-service... By default the service runs an embedded WebDriver server inside your app... and this is how macOS is supported.
impact: The earlier conclusion that macOS Tauri applications lack practical first-party-aligned desktop automation is obsolete.
implication: Keep Tauri and establish a real macOS native-shell WDIO lane; negative control: a deliberate selector or startup failure must make that lane fail.
notes: The older direct tauri-driver route remains Windows/Linux only.

subject: WDIO Tauri integration
claim: Embedded native testing and advanced Tauri-aware testing require separate plugins: tauri-plugin-wdio-webdriver supplies the in-app WebDriver server, while tauri-plugin-wdio plus @wdio/tauri-plugin supplies execute, IPC mocks, and log forwarding.
evidence_quote: tauri-plugin-wdio: Execute API, mocking, log forwarding; tauri-plugin-wdio-webdriver: Embedded WebDriver server, only for embedded provider.
impact: Installing only the driver plugin yields DOM automation but not the Tauri bridge features the harness may assume.
implication: Model plugin installation, Rust registration, frontend import, capabilities, and a plugin-availability smoke as one atomic roadmap unit; negative control: omit each half independently and require the corresponding smoke to fail clearly.
notes: Basic WebDriver element operations can work without tauri-plugin-wdio, so the smoke must test browser.tauri.execute or logging, not only a click.

subject: WDIO browser mode
claim: WDIO browser mode runs the renderer in Chrome against a dev server and intercepts Tauri invoke calls, but it does not launch a Tauri binary or exercise real Rust command round-trips, window management, or deeplinks.
evidence_quote: Browser mode is a frontend-only test mode... the Tauri Rust backend is replaced by mocks... It is not suitable when your tests need... real command round-trips to a running Rust backend.
impact: Browser-mode success cannot be used as evidence that Vean's shell, WKWebView, Rust setup, native menu, or sidecar supervisor works.
implication: Use browser mode only for fast renderer contract tests and label it explicitly; negative control: a deliberately broken Rust command must leave browser-mode tests green but fail native mode.
notes: The service can start and stop a Vite dev server and tear down its process tree.

subject: IPC mock behavior
claim: In browser mode every unmocked Tauri command throws, mocks reset their implementation and call history when re-created, and event simulation has explicit limitations.
evidence_quote: Patches window.__TAURI_INTERNALS__.invoke... throws if the command has no registered mock... mock(command) fully resets... Other plugin commands no-op resolve to undefined unless you explicitly mock them.
impact: The mode can enforce renderer/backend contract completeness, but careless global fixtures or unsupported plugin assumptions can create false confidence.
implication: Declare mocks per test, reset automatically, and add an unmocked-command negative control; do not hide unknown invokes behind a permissive catch-all mock.
notes: Element click/setValue operations automatically synchronize active mock call data.

subject: native-mode backend access
claim: Native mode can execute code with Tauri APIs and can either call the real backend or replace selected invokes through JavaScript-side interception, so real-IPC and mocked-IPC cases must be distinct tests.
evidence_quote: browser.tauri.execute(): Execute arbitrary JavaScript in the Tauri frontend context with access to Tauri APIs... Command mocking... is implemented via JavaScript-side invoke interception.
impact: A test using browser.tauri.mock proves frontend behavior and call shape, not Rust implementation behavior, even though a real app is running.
implication: Reserve at least one unmocked native test for preview_port/run_action and backend failure propagation; negative control: break the Rust handler and require the real-IPC case to fail while the mocked case remains isolated.
notes: The distinction should be encoded in suite names and evidence output.

subject: application logs
claim: Frontend and backend log capture are both disabled by default and have separate level filters, while embedded startup failures specifically recommend enabling backend capture to expose app stderr.
evidence_quote: captureBackendLogs default: false... captureFrontendLogs default: false... Set captureBackendLogs: true... to see the app's stderr.
impact: Without explicit configuration a failed Vean sidecar launch or frontend exception will produce weak CI diagnostics.
implication: Enable warn-or-higher capture normally and debug capture on retry, persist outputDir artifacts, and fail on agreed fatal signatures; negative control: emit a sentinel stderr line and console error and prove both appear in the artifact.
notes: Capturing all logs adds overhead, so filtering is preferable to disabling evidence.

subject: embedded WebDriver exposure
claim: The desktop embedded driver binds its full WebDriver router to 127.0.0.1, while the router includes session creation and script-evaluation endpoints and no authentication middleware is present in the route construction.
evidence_quote: On other platforms, bind to localhost only for security; SocketAddr::from(([127, 0, 0, 1], port)); router exposes /wdio/eval and /session.
impact: Loopback reduces network exposure but any local process could attempt to control a test-enabled Vean instance, so shipping the plugin would create an unacceptable local automation surface.
implication: Compile the driver only behind a dedicated test feature/profile and add a release-binary symbol/port probe negative control proving no WDIO server starts even when TAURI_WEBDRIVER_PORT is set.
notes: This security conclusion is derived from the pinned router and server source; it is stronger than relying on runtime convention alone.

subject: test-only plugins
claim: WebdriverIO explicitly says tauri-plugin-wdio is test-only and demonstrates conditional debug registration or an optional Cargo feature, while it places tauri-plugin-wdio-webdriver under debug-assertion dependencies.
evidence_quote: Should I Include the Plugin in Production? No, the plugin is test-only... [target.'cfg(debug_assertions)'.dependencies] tauri-plugin-wdio-webdriver = '1'.
impact: A normal Tauri release build will intentionally lack the embedded driver, so the same binary cannot simultaneously be the production artifact and the embedded-WDIO test target.
implication: Create separate test-instrumented and production builds, then prove the production artifact separately; negative control: dependency/tree and runtime probes must reject either WDIO plugin in the signed distribution.
notes: A feature-gated release-like test build can validate release optimizations, but it is not the final distributable artifact.

subject: dependency maturity
claim: The stable Tauri service is only about two months old: v1.0.0 shipped May 3, 2026 and current v1.2.0 shipped June 25, 2026.
evidence_quote: @wdio/tauri-service: v1.0.0 released 03 May; wdio-tauri-service@v1.2.0 tagged 25 Jun.
impact: The capability is credible and upstream-tested but still likely to change and expose early integration defects.
implication: Pin @wdio/tauri-service, tauri-plugin-wdio, and tauri-plugin-wdio-webdriver to 1.2.0 initially, record a known-good matrix, and use a scheduled dependency-upgrade lane rather than floating ranges; negative control: lockfile drift check fails on unreviewed major/minor movement.
notes: Current upstream main at the time of research was commit ea78b1ad dated 2026-07-03.

subject: Vean dependency alignment
claim: Vean is locked to Tauri 2.11.3, satisfying tauri-plugin-wdio-webdriver 1.2.0's Tauri 2.10 minimum, while @wdio/tauri-service 1.2.0 requires WebdriverIO 9 and Node 18.12+/20.9+/22.11+.
evidence_quote: Cargo.lock: tauri 2.11.3; plugin Cargo.toml: tauri 2.10.0; service package.json peerDependencies: webdriverio ^9.0.0 and engines ^18.12.0 || ^20.9.0 || >=22.11.0.
impact: There is no Tauri-version blocker, but Vean must introduce and pin a Node-based WDIO 9 toolchain rather than assuming its existing Bun/Vitest 2 stack supplies it.
implication: Use Node 24 in CI, keep installation lockfile-backed, and add version-report output to evidence; negative control: unsupported Node execution must fail in preflight before building the app.
notes: The app package's manifest ranges resolve newer than their written ^2.9.0 values, making lockfile state the relevant compatibility evidence.

subject: driver provider selection
claim: Current service source treats embedded as the default when driverProvider is unset, but a current troubleshooting page still says Windows/Linux do not default, so relying on auto-detection is unnecessarily ambiguous.
evidence_quote: isEmbeddedProvider: if options.driverProvider return comparison; otherwise return true; troubleshooting says Windows and Linux do not default to any driver provider automatically.
impact: Mutable documentation currently disagrees with released implementation behavior.
implication: Set driverProvider: 'embedded' explicitly in every Vean native capability and pin versions; negative control: configuration schema test rejects an omitted or unknown provider.
notes: This is a documented-source contradiction, not a product blocker.

subject: parallel execution
claim: WebdriverIO's own standard embedded Tauri suite forces maxInstances to 1 because one app instance shares global window.__wdio_mocks__ state across workers, while multiremote allocates distinct ports and instances deliberately.
evidence_quote: Sequential execution required: embedded mode shares a single Tauri app instance across all workers, so window.__wdio_mocks__ is global shared state... maxInstances = 1.
impact: Turning on parallelism by default can make tests order-dependent even before considering Vean's shared ~/.vean state and sidecar ports.
implication: Start the native shell lane serially with an isolated HOME and per-test project fixtures; only add parallelism after process, port, and state isolation tests pass; negative control: two conflicting fixtures must never observe each other's project or mocks.
notes: Browser-mode and pure renderer tests can remain parallel because they do not share the native app instance.

subject: macOS CI feasibility
claim: Upstream runs embedded Tauri E2E on GitHub-hosted macos-latest ARM runners with Node 24 across standard, window, multiremote, standalone, and deeplink matrices.
evidence_quote: E2E - Tauri [macOS-ARM]... matrix test-type: standard, window, multiremote, standalone, deeplink; os: macos-latest; node-version: 24.
impact: A macOS CI lane is technically feasible without a paid CrabNebula driver or a self-hosted interactive Mac.
implication: Adopt macos-latest for Vean's native-shell gate, cache Cargo/Bun/Node dependencies, and retain artifacts on failure; negative control: run a known-bad binary once during harness validation and prove CI reports the startup error.
notes: Vean's large sidecar artifacts may make caching and job duration more significant than upstream's fixture app.

subject: process cleanup
claim: Upstream explicitly kills fixture app processes before and after embedded tests instead of trusting service teardown alone.
evidence_quote: Cleanup before Embedded provider tests... pkill -9 -f tauri-e2e-app; Post-cleanup Embedded... pkill -9 -f tauri-e2e-app.
impact: Process leakage is a recognized practical CI risk in the reference implementation.
implication: Vean needs deterministic PID/port tracking plus an after-suite orphan audit for vean-app, Bun preview, melt, ffmpeg, and ffprobe; negative control: intentionally leak a child and require the audit to fail before cleanup.
notes: Cleanup should avoid broad pkill patterns on developer machines; use test-specific HOME, markers, PIDs, and ports.

subject: Vean sidecar teardown
claim: The embedded provider terminates the Tauri app with SIGTERM and escalates to SIGKILL after five seconds, while Vean relies on Rust Drop to kill its Bun child, so orphaning the preview process is a concrete risk that must be measured rather than assumed away.
evidence_quote: child.kill('SIGTERM')... after gracefulTimeout 5000... child.kill('SIGKILL'); Vean Sidecar Drop calls child.kill().
impact: OS signal termination may bypass normal Rust destruction, and the Bun preview can itself own media subprocesses and a listening port.
implication: Add a teardown contract that records child PIDs/port and proves they disappear after both normal window close and WDIO termination; negative control: a deliberately detached child must be detected.
notes: The orphan outcome depends on platform and app signal handling, so the roadmap should begin with an empirical spike and then harden ownership if it reproduces.

subject: embedded server port
claim: The embedded server defaults to fixed port 4445 and reads TAURI_WEBDRIVER_PORT, while the service supports explicit embeddedPort and unique ports for multiple instances.
evidence_quote: DEFAULT_PORT: u16 = 4445; PORT_ENV_VAR = TAURI_WEBDRIVER_PORT; port is environment value or default.
impact: Concurrent local sessions or stale processes can create confusing startup failures or connect a test to the wrong app.
implication: Allocate a free test port per run, pass it explicitly, verify the server PID owns it, and fail closed on collision; negative control: pre-bind the chosen port and require a diagnostic collision failure.
notes: Do not use port 0 unless the plugin and service jointly support discovering the selected port; allocate and reserve carefully.

subject: Vean remote viewer architecture
claim: Vean navigates its main webview from the bundled splash to a random localhost viewer origin, so importing @wdio/tauri-plugin only in the app entry page would be lost on navigation and advanced browser.tauri APIs would not initialize in the final page.
evidence_quote: navigate_to_sidecar calls window.navigate(http://127.0.0.1:{port}/); WDIO docs require importing @wdio/tauri-plugin in the frontend entry before tests.
impact: A stock WDIO plugin recipe is insufficient for Vean's full-window remote navigation architecture.
implication: Prototype conditional test instrumentation in the served viewer or constrain the first native lane to plain WebDriver DOM operations; negative control: wait until the final random-port URL and assert plugin availability there, not on the splash.
notes: Any remote-origin capability added for testing must be test-only and least-privileged.

subject: Tauri remote-origin authority
claim: Vean currently has no explicit capability file, withGlobalTauri setting, or WDIO permissions, while advanced WDIO setup requires global Tauri and wdio capabilities; granting those to the localhost viewer would expand authority for remote content.
evidence_quote: Current app.security contains only csp: null; WDIO setup requires withGlobalTauri: true and wdio permissions in a capability.
impact: Carelessly following the generic recipe could weaken the production trust boundary around a mutable localhost HTTP origin.
implication: Use a separate test configuration/capability scoped to the exact test URL pattern and prove production config lacks withGlobalTauri and wdio permissions; negative control: production viewer attempts to invoke a WDIO command and is denied.
notes: The random port makes exact URL scoping awkward and should be addressed in the test configuration design, not by allowing broad remote origins in production.

subject: native menu and dialog automation
claim: The Tauri service drives webview content and Tauri webview windows, whereas WebdriverIO documents Appium Mac2 as the route for arbitrary macOS-native application elements such as menus and system dialogs.
evidence_quote: WebdriverIO can automate arbitrary MacOS application using Appium... XCode, Appium and the Mac2 Driver... selectors such as XCUIElementTypeButton.
impact: Embedded Tauri WDIO will not by itself prove Vean's File menu, keyboard accelerator, Finder folder picker, or blocking native message dialogs.
implication: Add a very thin Appium Mac2 or equivalent macOS-native smoke for menu/dialog flows after the webview lane is stable; negative control: rename a native menu item or break Cmd+O routing and require the native smoke to fail.
notes: Do not duplicate renderer coverage in Appium; keep this lane limited to surfaces outside the webview.

subject: accessibility automation
claim: WebdriverIO's documented axe integration analyzes a web page through the browser object, making it appropriate for Vean's viewer DOM but not a substitute for macOS accessibility-tree validation of native menus and dialogs.
evidence_quote: Initialize AxeBuilder with the browser object and call builder.analyze() on a page.
impact: A clean axe report would cover only one half of Vean's hybrid UI accessibility surface.
implication: Run axe in browser and native webview lanes, then separately inspect native controls through Appium/Accessibility APIs; negative control: introduce a DOM accessibility violation and a native unlabeled control independently and require different gates to catch them.
notes: Computed role/label WebDriver endpoints can supplement DOM assertions but still operate inside the webview.

subject: Tauri frontend mocks
claim: Tauri's @tauri-apps/api/mocks can intercept invoke, simulate selected events and fake window labels in frontend tests, but no real webview or Rust backend runs and window properties are not simulated automatically.
evidence_quote: mockIPC fakes invoke under the mock runtime — no real webview or Rust backend runs... mockWindows only fakes the existence of windows but no window properties.
impact: These mocks are fast and useful for contract tests but cannot validate Vean's actual Rust sidecar supervisor or WKWebView navigation.
implication: Use them beneath WDIO rather than instead of WDIO; negative control: a Rust regression must be invisible to the mock unit and caught by a higher native layer.
notes: Tauri also warns to clear mocks after every test to prevent state leakage.

subject: bundled media sidecars
claim: Tauri externalBin packaging requires architecture-suffixed source binaries and strips/locates them by configured names, while JavaScript shell execution additionally requires explicit allow-execute or allow-spawn scopes.
evidence_quote: A binary with the same name and a -$TARGET_TRIPLE suffix must exist... externalBin... shell:allow-execute or shell:allow-spawn.
impact: A raw debug-binary WDIO pass cannot prove Vean's packaged melt/ffmpeg/ffprobe placement, target architecture, dylib closure, permissions, or execution from the .app.
implication: Add a separate packaged-app smoke launched from Contents/MacOS with a clean environment and require a real probe/render through all three sidecars; negative control: remove or rename one bundled binary and require the package proof to fail.
notes: Vean currently launches its Bun core directly from Rust rather than using Tauri's shell plugin, so its lifecycle and authority model needs its own explicit proof.

subject: Vean installed-app independence
claim: Vean's shell defaults to executing bun against a source-tree src/cli.ts and uses VEAN_REPO/VEAN_BIN overrides, so a native WDIO run from the repository is not evidence that a downloaded .app works without Bun, source, Homebrew, or existing ~/.vean state.
evidence_quote: vean_bin defaults to 'bun'; spawn_sidecar builds vean_repo()/src/cli.ts; packaged mode only changes preview --prod and renderer binary paths.
impact: The current packaging story may be non-standalone even though Tauri successfully emits an app or DMG.
implication: Make clean-machine independence an early release gate: sanitized HOME and PATH, no VEAN_REPO/VEAN_BIN, quarantined .app, real preview and render; negative control: hide Bun/source/Homebrew and require the current artifact to expose the gap until fixed.
notes: This is a release blocker/path item for the harness roadmap, not evidence that Tauri itself should be replaced.

subject: macOS distribution
claim: A macOS app distributed outside the App Store requires Developer ID signing and notarization, and Tauri supports CI credentials and bundle commands but does not make an unsigned build equivalent to a distributable artifact.
evidence_quote: Code signing is required... when downloaded from the browser... Notarization is required when using a Developer ID Application certificate.
impact: WDIO against a debug or ad-hoc-signed binary cannot close Vean's release assurance claim.
implication: Create a release lane that signs, notarizes, staples, verifies codesign and Gatekeeper assessment, then smoke-launches the exact artifact; negative control: tamper with a nested sidecar after signing and require signature verification to fail.
notes: Vean currently ad-hoc signs collected sidecars during bundling, which is not the same as final Developer ID signing of the nested code and app.

subject: application updates
claim: Tauri updater artifacts require a separate cryptographic signature that cannot be disabled, and losing the updater private key prevents publishing future updates to installed users.
evidence_quote: Tauri's updater needs a signature... This cannot be disabled... if you lose this key you will NOT be able to publish new updates to users that have the app already installed.
impact: Apple signing alone does not prove update integrity or operability, and Vean currently has no updater configuration.
implication: Treat updater enablement as a later explicit release unit with key escrow, signed fixture update, rollback/no-update tests, and a tampered-signature negative control; do not claim it from the initial Tauri harness.
notes: The updater enforces TLS in production unless dangerous insecure transport is explicitly enabled.

subject: test artifact identity
claim: Current @wdio/tauri-service trusts an explicit appBinaryPath and removed project-directory auto-resolution because Cargo workspaces, release layouts, and product-name/package-name mismatches made inference unreliable.
evidence_quote: The service trusts this path as-is... it does not try to resolve a binary from a project root... resolver removed because it broke for Cargo workspaces, release builds, and productName-vs-Cargo-package-name mismatches.
impact: Tests can silently target the wrong debug/release/package artifact if Vean derives paths informally.
implication: Have the build step emit an absolute artifact manifest with hash and pass that exact binary to WDIO; negative control: point at a stale binary and require hash/build-id mismatch before session creation.
notes: For a packaged .app the executable path and the bundle path should both be recorded.

subject: instrumented binary identity
claim: WebdriverIO's own embedded suite intentionally resolves debug Tauri builds, confirming that embedded native E2E and final package verification are separate evidence layers.
evidence_quote: Uses debug builds — they include tauri-plugin-automation needed by the CrabNebula provider... resolveTauriFixtureBinary(appPath).
impact: Calling the embedded lane a packaged-production test would overstate what was exercised.
implication: Name lanes native-instrumented and packaged-production, require both before release, and preserve hashes proving which binary each ran; negative control: ensure the production package has no embedded driver while the instrumented binary remains controllable.
notes: A release-optimized test profile may be added, but final signing/notarization still occurs on a non-instrumented artifact.

subject: framework decision
claim: The combined service features—real cross-platform app launch, WebDriver DOM interaction, Tauri API execution, IPC mocking, logs, multiple windows, and macOS embedded support—close the main testability argument for replacing Tauri, while leaving bounded native-menu and release-proof gaps that have complementary tools.
evidence_quote: Cross-platform E2E testing... full Tauri API access... mocking... backend and frontend log capture... multiremote... browser mode.
impact: The evidence supports staying on Tauri rather than undertaking a UI-framework migration solely for automation quality.
implication: Roadmap the layered harness first and revisit framework choice only if the native-shell spike fails its explicit go/no-go gates; negative control: record any unsupported critical Vean flow rather than masking it with a mocked lower layer.
notes: This conclusion does not claim WDIO covers video correctness, Remotion behavior, OS-native controls, or distribution integrity by itself.
